channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Scientists improve knowledge on sea level rise—and confirm it has been accelerating since 1960 - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Scientists improve knowledge on sea level rise—and confirm it has been accelerating since 1960  Phys.org

  3. Sea Level Rise is Accelerating, Scientists Confirm  eos.org
  4. Sea levels may keep rising for centuries due to low clouds  Earth.com
  5. Less low cloud cover lets in more heat from the sun—and may lock in centuries of sea level rise  Phys.org

item.title: A few rocks spotted near a pond in northeastern Thailand in 2016 have just been confirmed as a 27-tonne, 27-metre sauropod — the largest dinosaur ever found in Southeast Asia - Space Daily
item.description:

  3. A new species offers a clue to the boom of giant dinosaurs  National Geographic

item.title: Astronauts on the ISS lose about 1-2% of their bone density per month in microgravity — meaning a six-month mission costs them as much bone mass as a postmenopausal woman loses in a year — and the countermeasures NASA developed to slow that loss - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Astronauts on the ISS lose about 1-2% of their bone density per month in microgravity — meaning a six-month mission costs them as much bone mass as a postmenopausal woman loses in a year — and the countermeasures NASA developed to slow that loss  Space Daily
  2. Space physiology and technology: Adaptations, countermeasures, and opportunities for wearable systems  EurekAlert!

item.title: The Mars rovers carry no clocks set to Earth time, so the engineers driving them shifted their entire lives to a 24-hour-39-minute Martian day, and within weeks JPL staff were sleeping during California afternoons, eating breakfast at midnight, and quietly develo - Space Daily
item.description: The Mars rovers carry no clocks set to Earth time, so the engineers driving them shifted their entire lives to a 24-hour-39-minute Martian day, and within weeks JPL staff were sleeping during California afternoons, eating breakfast at midnight, and quietly develo  Space Daily

item.title: The most accurate atomic clocks in operation now lose less than one second every 30 billion years — and the reason this matters isn't precision for its own sake, it's that gravity itself slows time slightly, and these clocks are now sensitive enough to measure the - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The most accurate atomic clocks in operation now lose less than one second every 30 billion years — and the reason this matters isn't precision for its own sake, it's that gravity itself slows time slightly, and these clocks are now sensitive enough to measure the  Space Daily
  2. Schrödinger’s clock: Time could tick faster and slower at the same time  ScienceDaily
  3. "Different Flows of Time All Exist at the Same Moment": Scientists Claim Trapped-Ion Atomic Clocks Can Observe “Quantum Superposition of Time”  The Debrief
  4. New atomic clocks can be used to observe the 'quantum superposition of time'  Earth.com
  5. Using atomic clocks to reveal the quantum properties of time  EurekAlert!

item.title: Scott Kelly spent a year in orbit while his identical twin brother stayed on Earth, and when he came home NASA discovered his gene expression had changed in ways that didn't fully reverse - Space Daily
item.description:

  3. The ISS travels at 17,500 miles per hour, which means astronauts inside it are aging measurably slower than people on the ground, and by the time Scott Kelly returned from his year in orbit he was 5 milliseconds younger than his identical twin brother Mark.  Space Daily
  4. The International Space Station is travelling at 17,500 mph as you read this, and the astronauts inside it are aging measurably slower than you are  Space Daily
